teamObjective.explanation: As the opening leader I want to start the trick cheaply. A low spade lead lets the trick develop without committing strength, and if my partner can cover it we retain control.

partnerBelief.summary: My partner has shown no cards, so their strength is unknown; they are somewhat likely to hold a card that can beat a low spade, but this is uncertain.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents have shown no cards. Each opponent may hold higher spades, trumps, or nothing relevant, but I cannot assert any specific holding.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of spades is the cheapest way to open the trick. It preserves my 4, 5, and 6 for later positions where they might beat lower opposing cards, and it gives my partner the widest range of cards that can win the trick without requiring them to spend high cards.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4 is higher than needed for an opening lead and is more useful as a covering card later.
The 5 can already beat a 3 or 4; leading it wastes covering power and leaves me with weaker remaining cards.
The 6 is my highest non-trump card and my best chance to win or cover a trick later; it is too valuable to lead.

risks: An opponent or partner may play a higher card or a trump and win the trick, so I may lose the lead immediately.
With only low spades and no trumps, regaining the lead later could be difficult if I lose it now.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the minimum card minimizes the value given away and keeps my slightly higher cards available for future tricks.

notes: Opening lead from a very weak hand; the default is to lead the lowest available card and conserve covering cards.
